claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 18 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 17 and in approved education; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 17 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
circumstances: Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To begin, I need to ask you about the child (or children) you're claiming for:

**How old is your child?**

Please tell me their age, as this determines which eligibility path we follow:
- **Under 16**: They may automatically qualify
- **16-19**: We'll need to check if they're in approved education or training
- **20 or over**: Unfortunately, they wouldn't qualify

If you have multiple children, let's start with one child at a time, and we can assess each one.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I have two children actually - Alex who's 17, and Blake who's 18. Should I tell you about both of them or do you want to go through them one at a time?

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I can help you with both Alex (17) and Blake (18). Since they're both in the 16-19 age range, we'll need to check their education or training status for each of them.

Let's go through them one at a time to make sure we get all the details right. We'll start with Alex, then move on to Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Starting with Alex (age 17):**

Is Alex currently:
1. **In approved education or training** (currently enrolled and studying), OR
2. **Recently left education or training** (stopped within the last 20 weeks), OR
3. **Neither** of these?

This is important because for 16-19 year olds, Child Benefit typically continues only if they're still in approved education or training, or have just recently left.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is still in education - he's studying for his A-levels at the moment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Since Alex is currently in education studying for A-levels, let's verify that this qualifies as approved education.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great news! A-levels are approved (non-advanced) education. Now I need to check a few more details about Alex's studies:

1. **Is Alex studying at least 12 hours per week of supervised study?** (This typically includes classroom time, lectures, and supervised coursework - not homework done alone at home)

2. **Is this education funded or arranged by an employer**, or is there an agreement with an employer for a job after completion?

3. **Is Alex married or in a civil partnership?** (If yes, we need to check if they're financially dependent on you)

For typical A-level students attending sixth form or college, the answer is usually: yes to enough hours, no to employer funding, and no to being married. Can you confirm these details for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he's doing full-time A-levels at sixth form college, so definitely more than 12 hours a week of classes. No, there's no employer involved - it's just regular school. And no, he's not married or anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Alex's A-level studies clearly qualify as approved education. Now I need to check one more thing for Alex:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Alex:** Is he working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?

This is important because even if a young person is in approved education, if they're working 24 hours or more per week, they wouldn't qualify for Child Benefit.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't work at all - he's just focused on his studies at the moment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That's fine for Alex. Now let me check the next requirement:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Alex:** Is he receiving any benefits in his own right, such as Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit?

Most students don't receive these benefits, so the answer is usually no.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't get any benefits in his own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! One more question about Alex's education:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Alex:** Is he doing an apprenticeship?

You mentioned he's doing A-levels at sixth form college, so I assume the answer is no, but I need to confirm as apprenticeships have different rules depending on the type and location.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not doing an apprenticeship - just his A-levels.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Alex looks good so far. Now let's move on to **Blake (age 18)**.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Before we move on to Blake, I need to ask about **your residency status** (this applies to both children):

**Do you normally live in the UK?**

Most claimants do, but if not, there are other ways to meet the residency requirement (such as having settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, or being a Crown servant abroad).

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - we're in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now let's move on to **Blake (age 18)**. 

Since Blake is also in the 16-19 age range, I need to ask similar questions:

**Is Blake currently:**
1. **In approved education or training** (currently enrolled and studying), OR
2. **Recently left education or training** (stopped within the last 20 weeks), OR
3. **Neither** of these?

What's Blake's current situation regarding education or training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake isn't in education anymore - he finished school and isn't studying at the moment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see. Blake has left education. Let me check if he might still qualify under the extension period:

**When did Blake leave education or training?** 

If it's been within the last 20 weeks, he may still be eligible for Child Benefit during what's called an "extension period" until a specific date. If it's been longer than 20 weeks, then unfortunately he wouldn't qualify as a 16-19 year old needs to be in approved education/training to be eligible.

Can you tell me approximately when Blake finished school?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been quite a while now - he left last year, so it's definitely been more than 20 weeks.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I understand. Unfortunately, since Blake left education more than 20 weeks ago and is not currently in approved education or training, he doesn't meet the qualifying criteria for Child Benefit as an 18-year-old.

Let me navigate to confirm this and then continue with the remaining questions about you and Alex:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Let me check where we are in the assessment. I need to continue with the claimant questions for Alex first, then we'll circle back to Blake's situation:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now, continuing with the questions that apply to both children:

**Are you responsible for Alex and Blake?**

This means either:
- You live with them, OR
- You contribute to their upkeep (paying for food, clothes, housing, etc.)

I assume you live with them both in Manchester?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, both Alex and Blake live at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Now let me check about other claimants:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex or Blake?**

For example, another parent, guardian, or family member? 

Only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child at a time, so if someone else is claiming, we'd need to know if they've agreed to transfer the claim to you.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for either of them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Now let me check where the children live:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Are Alex or Blake:**
- In hospital (for an extended period)?
- In local authority care?
- Being fostered?
- Or are they living normally at home with you?

From what you've told me, they both live at home with you in Manchester, so I assume they're living normally at home?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - they're both just living at home with me normally. Neither is in hospital, care, or being fostered.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
One final question:

**Is the local council paying towards Alex or Blake's accommodation or maintenance?**

For example, paying for their housing costs or contributing to their upkeep. Most families supporting their children at home don't receive such payments, so the answer is usually no.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything towards either of them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment Results

Thank you for providing all that information. I've completed the assessment for both of your children. Here's the outcome:

---

### **Alex (age 17): ✅ ELIGIBLE**

**Good news!** You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex because:
- Alex is 17 and in approved non-advanced education (A-levels at sixth form college)
- He's studying full-time with more than 12 hours per week of supervised study
- He's not working 24+ hours per week
- He's not receiving benefits in his own right
- He's not in a disqualifying apprenticeship
- You meet all the claimant requirements (UK residency, living with Alex, responsible for him, no one else claiming, council not paying)

---

### **Blake (age 18): ❌ NOT ELIGIBLE**

Unfortunately, you cannot claim Child Benefit for Blake because:
- Blake is 18 and not currently in approved education or training
- He left education last year (more than 20 weeks ago), so he doesn't qualify for the extension period
- For 16-19 year olds to be eligible, they must be either in approved education/training OR have left within the last 20 weeks

**However**, if Blake returns to approved education or training (such as college courses, A-levels, T-levels, or similar non-advanced qualifications), he could become eligible again as long as he remains under 20 years old.

---

### **Next Steps for Alex:**

1. **Apply for Child Benefit** online at gov.uk or by post using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate
4. **Be aware of the High Income Child Benefit Charge**: If you or your partner earn over £60,000 per year, you may need to pay some or all of the Child Benefit back through tax
5. **Payments** are typically made every 4 weeks

You can apply here: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

Do you have any questions about this assessment or the next steps?

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and in approved non-advanced education (A-levels at sixth form college), studying full-time with more than 12 hours per week of supervised study. Alex is not working 24+ hours per week, is not receiving benefits in his own right, and is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester), lives with Alex, is responsible for him, and no one else is claiming for him. Alex is living at home normally and the council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance. Alex meets all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.

Blake is 18 years old and not currently in approved education or training. Blake left education last year, which is more than 20 weeks ago, so does not qualify for the extension period available to those who have recently left education. For 16-19 year olds to qualify for Child Benefit, they must be in approved education or training, or have left within the last 20 weeks. Since Blake meets neither of these conditions, Blake does not qualify as a qualifying child for Child Benefit purposes.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children aged 17 and 18. Alex (17) qualifies because he is in full-time approved education (A-levels) and meets all other eligibility requirements. Blake (18) does not qualify because he left education more than 20 weeks ago and is not currently in approved education or training, which is required for 16-19 year olds to be eligible for Child Benefit.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.question: Is the child in approved (non-advanced) education or training?
response.help_text: Approved education: A-levels, T-levels, NVQ Level 3 or below, Scottish Highers/Advanced Highers. Advanced (NOT approved): University degrees, HNC, HND, NVQ Level 4+. Must be full-time (12+ hours/week supervised study).

criteria.must_not_be_married_unless_dependent: Married 16-19 year olds only qualify if financially dependent on claimant

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 17 years old and in approved non-advanced education (A-levels at sixth form college), studying full-time with more than 12 hours per week of supervised study. Alex is not working 24+ hours per week, is not receiving benefits in his own right, and is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester), lives with Alex, is responsible for him, and no one else is claiming for him. Alex is living at home normally and the council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance. Alex meets all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.

Blake is 18 years old and not currently in approved education or training. Blake left education last year, which is more than 20 weeks ago, so does not qualify for the extension period available to those who have recently left education. For 16-19 year olds to qualify for Child Benefit, they must be in approved education or training, or have left within the last 20 weeks. Since Blake meets neither of these conditions, Blake does not qualify as a qualifying child for Child Benefit purposes.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children aged 17 and 18. Alex (17) qualifies because he is in full-time approved education (A-levels) and meets all other eligibility requirements. Blake (18) does not qualify because he left education more than 20 weeks ago and is not currently in approved education or training, which is required for 16-19 year olds to be eligible for Child Benefit.
